sources.notes: Statement and constraints: one slide operation can reattach a subtree; make the tree a path or output -1.
Official editorial confirms using the diameter criterion and sliding the unique off-diameter branch to a diameter endpoint.

annotation.summary: Given a tree, output at most one valid slide operation that makes the tree a simple path; if it already is a path, output -1.
annotation.constraints: sum n <= 200000; linear diameter/path recovery is needed.
annotation.core_idea: One slide can increase the diameter by at most 1. To become a path of length n-1 after one operation, the current diameter must be n-2; if it is n-1, no operation is needed. Recover a diameter path. When the length is n-2, exactly one vertex lies outside the diameter path and is attached to a diameter vertex. Slide that off-path vertex/subtree to the farther diameter endpoint so it becomes the new path endpoint.
tricks: Use the diameter length as the feasibility criterion.
A feasible non-path tree has exactly one off-diameter vertex.
Slide the unique branch to a diameter endpoint.

solution_variants.name: diameter-one-slide-construction
solution_variants.summary: Find a diameter by two DFS/BFS passes; if its length is n-2, locate the unique off-path neighbor and reattach it to an endpoint.

tags.evidence: The possibility of a one-slide construction is determined by whether the current tree diameter is n-2 or n-1.
The solution must output a concrete slide triple that turns the unique off-path branch into a path endpoint.
The operation and proof depend on tree subtrees, diameter path, and off-path branch structure.
Implementation uses DFS/BFS to find diameter endpoints, restore the diameter path, and mark path vertices.
